content.content:
Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSAV-ark_vouch.adoc
Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSAC-ark_conjure.adoc

1	//axhob_operation
2	{rbtgo_ark_conjure}
3	
4	NOTE: This operation conjures an
5	{rbtga_ark_image}
6	in
7	{rbtgi_repository}
8	by building a container image from a vessel specification. The
9	{rbtr_director}
10	performs the build via
11	{gcb_service}
12	using direct `builds.create` API submission.
13	
14	NOTE: Conjure produces both the image and about artifacts in a single Cloud Build job submitted via `builds.create`.
15	The about steps (`rbgja01`-`rbgja04`) are embedded via stitch alongside the build steps.
16	The vouch artifact is created by the subsequent {rbtgo_ark_vouch} pipeline stage (always a separate Cloud Build job).
17	
18	NOTE: The
19	{rbtga_hallmark}
20	is minted locally by the
21	{rbtr_director}
22	before submission.
23	The Director knows the complete artifact tag set before the build begins -- naming authority and verification authority are the same entity.
24	
25	NOTE: The conjure build job runs on the worker pool selected by the vessel's `RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`: tether pool for vessels needing public internet (e.g., `apt-get` in Dockerfile), airgap pool for vessels with all dependencies enshrined in GAR.
26	The subsequent {rbtgo_ark_vouch} always runs on the airgap pool regardless of vessel egress mode.
27	
28	NOTE: *Universal reliquary precondition*. Every ordain-path vessel — conjure, bind, and graft — must have `RBRV_RELIQUARY` non-empty before this operation runs. The about/vouch pipeline (gcloud, docker, alpine, syft) consumes reliquary tool images uniformly across all modes; the registry preflight below enforces this.
29	
30	NOTE: The conjure Cloud Build job carries the {rbsk_in_pool_preflight} as its step 0, ahead of the extract-context step. Conjure populates `_RBGR_ENSHRINE_LOCATOR_n` substitutions from `RBRV_IMAGE_n_ANCHOR` slots, so the preflight HEAD-checks each anchored base in addition to the reliquary tools.
31	
32	The conjure operation:
33	
34	//axhopt_typed_parameter
35	{rbtga_ark}
36	. *Validate Input Parameters*:
37	.. {rbbc_require}
38	vessel provided as first argument
39	.. {rbbc_require}
40	vessel contains conjuring configuration (RBRV_CONJURE_DOCKERFILE, RBRV_CONJURE_BLDCONTEXT)
41	
42	//axhos_step
43	. *Registry Preflight*:
44	.. *Reliquary check*: HEAD request to `«RELIQUARY»/docker:latest` manifest in
45	{gar_service}.
46	{rbbc_require}
47	HTTP 200 — on 404, fail with directive to run inscribe first.
48	The reliquary is a co-versioned set of builder tool images inscribed from upstream into private GAR. Each vessel declares its reliquary; multiple datestamped reliquaries can coexist.
49	.. *Enshrine check*: For each indexed slot (n = 1, 2, 3) where both
50	{rbrv_image_origin}
51	and
52	{rbrv_image_anchor}
53	are set:
54	... Parse
55	{rbrv_image_anchor}
56	as a
57	{rbst_locator}
58	into package-path and tag
59	... HEAD request to `«LOCATOR-PATH»:«LOCATOR-TAG»` manifest in
60	{gar_service}
61	... {rbbc_require}
62	HTTP 200 — on 404, fail with directive to run {rbtgo_ark_enshrine} first.
63	Multiple vessels sharing the same anchor need only one enshrine.
64	.. Preflight prevents wasted
65	{gcb_service}
66	submission time on missing images, with copy-paste remediation commands
67	
68	//axhos_step
69	. *Resolve Base Images*:
70	.. For each indexed slot (n = 1, 2, 3) where
71	{rbrv_image_origin}
72	is set:
73	... If
74	{rbrv_image_anchor}
75	is present: parse the
76	{rbst_locator}
77	and resolve to full GAR image reference (`«GAR-REPO-BASE»/«LOCATOR-PATH»:«LOCATOR-TAG»`)
78	... If
79	{rbrv_image_anchor}
80	is absent: pass
81	{rbrv_image_origin}
82	through as the upstream tag (development path)
83	.. Resolved references become `RBF_IMAGE_n` build-args for the Dockerfile `ARG`/`FROM` pattern
84	
85	//axhos_step
86	. *Assign Hallmark*:
87	.. Mint
88	{rbtga_hallmark}
89	locally on the Director's workstation
90	.. {rbbc_store}
91	`«HALLMARK»` -- complete artifact tag set is now known before build submission
92	
93	//axhos_step
94	. *Push Pouch*:
95	.. Package build context (Dockerfile and context files) as a FROM SCRATCH OCI image
96	.. Push to GAR tagged `{gar_hallmarks_namespace}/«HALLMARK»/pouch:«HALLMARK»`
97	.. {rbbc_require}
98	push succeeds -- the pouch delivers build context to
99	{gcb_service}
100	
101	//axhos_step
102	. *Execute Build*:
103	.. Stitch
104	{rbtgr_build_json}
105	with all step image references and substitution variables
106	.. {rbbc_submit}
107	`POST «GCB_PROJECT_BUILDS_URL»` with Build resource JSON via `builds.create`
108	.. All build parameters in stitched
109	{rbtgr_build_json}
110	-- zero custom substitution overrides
111	.. Waits for build completion
112	.. On success, the
113	{rbtga_ark_image}
114	artifact is created with the
115	{rbtga_hallmark}
116	.. {rbtgr_provenance}
117	is auto-generated by Google as a signed attestation on the built image
118	
119	//axhoot_typed_output
120	{rbtga_ark_image}
121	
122	//axhoot_typed_output axd_fact
123	{rbf_fact_hallmark}
124	The full
125	{rbtga_hallmark}
126	string.
127	
128	//axhoot_typed_output axd_fact
129	{rbf_fact_build_id}
130	The
131	{gcb_build}
132	job ID from the `builds.create` submission.
133	Used by
134	{rbtgo_ark_vouch}
135	for
136	{rbtgr_provenance}
137	verification.
138	
139	//axhoot_typed_output axd_fact
140	{rbf_fact_gar_root}
141	{gar_service}
142	registry prefix.
143	Composable with
144	{rbf_fact_ark_stem}
145	for full artifact references.
146	
147	//axhoot_typed_output axd_fact
148	{rbf_fact_ark_stem}
149	Hallmark subtree prefix joining
150	{gar_service}
151	root with the hallmark path segment as `{gar_hallmarks_namespace}/«HALLMARK»`.
152	Composable with basename and `:«HALLMARK»` tag for full artifact references.
153	
154	//axhoot_typed_output axd_fact
155	{rbf_fact_ark_yield}
156	Per-platform
157	{rbtga_ark_image}
158	reference.
159	One fact file per platform discovered during the build.
160	
161	. *Display Results*:
162	.. {rbbc_show}
163	vessel image built with hallmark tag
164	
165	//axhoc_completion
166	Successful execution creates the
167	{rbtga_ark_image}
168	in
169	{rbtgi_repository}.
170	The {rbtr_director} retains `«BUILD_ID»` and `«HALLMARK»` from the conjure round-trip.
171	The `builds.create` job produces both the image and about artifacts under the hallmark subtree (about steps embedded via stitch).
172	{rbtgo_ark_ordain} chains only {rbtgo_ark_vouch} after the combined job completes, passing `«BUILD_ID»` for DSSE provenance verification.
173

1	//axhob_operation
2	{rbtgo_ark_vouch}
3	
4	NOTE: This operation produces the
5	{rbtga_ark_vouch}
6	artifact for an
7	{rbtga_ark}
8	in
9	{rbtgi_repository}
10	by running mode-aware verification in
11	{gcb_service}.
12	The
13	{rbtr_director}
14	submits a Cloud Build job via `builds.create` (direct submission), which runs as the
15	{rbtr_mason}
16	service account on the airgap private pool (`RBDC_POOL_AIRGAP`, derived from `RBDC_GCB_POOL_STEM` with `-airgap` suffix — no public egress).
17	
18	NOTE: Vouch uses `builds.create` rather than trigger dispatch because the
19	{rbtga_hallmark}
20	is known only after the image arrives in GAR.
21	The private pool matches conjure's execution environment.
22	
23	NOTE: The vouch verdict depends on vessel mode.
24	Conjure: DSSE envelope signature verification of GCB-signed provenance.
25	Bind: digest-pin comparison against the upstream reference.
26	Graft: GRAFTED stamp — explicit acknowledgment of no provenance chain.
27	
28	NOTE: Vouch participates in the {rbsk_in_pool_preflight} only when embedded in graft's combined about+vouch Cloud Build job (`zrbfv_graft_metadata_submit`). The standalone vouch submission (`zrbfv_vouch_submit`, the post-image stage of conjure and bind ordain flows) is out of scope per the premise.
29	
30	The
31	{rbtr_director}
32	vouches for an ark:
33	
34	//axhopt_typed_parameter
35	{rbtga_ark}
36	. *Validate Input Parameters*:
37	.. {rbbc_require}
38	vessel provided as first argument
39	.. {rbbc_require}
40	hallmark provided as second argument
41	
42	//axhos_step
43	. *Load Vessel Configuration*:
44	.. Source vessel configuration from `rbrv.env`
45	.. {rbbc_store}
46	`«VESSEL_MODE»` from {rbrv_vessel_mode}
47	
48	//axhos_step
49	. *Authenticate as
50	{rbtr_director}*:
51	.. Execute
52	{rbtoe_director_authenticate}
53	.. {rbbc_store}
54	`«DIRECTOR_TOKEN»` for subsequent API calls
55	
56	//axhos_step
57	. *Gate on About Existence*:
58	.. {rbbc_call}
59	Docker Registry API: HEAD manifests for `{gar_hallmarks_namespace}/«HALLMARK»/about:«HALLMARK»`
60	.. {rbbc_require}
61	HTTP 200 response -- {rbtgo_ark_about} must complete before vouch
62	.. If `{gar_hallmarks_namespace}/«HALLMARK»/vouch:«HALLMARK»` already exists:
63	{rbbc_warn}
64	re-vouch in progress (see idempotency below)
65	
66	//axhos_step
67	. *Submit Vouch Cloud Build*:
68	.. Construct Build resource JSON with `serviceAccount` as
69	{rbtr_mason},
70	substitution namespace `_RBGV_*`
71	.. Pass registry coordinates, vessel mode, and verify method as substitution variables
72	.. {rbbc_submit}
73	`POST «GCB_PROJECT_BUILDS_URL»` with Build resource JSON
74	.. {rbbc_require}
75	HTTP 200 response
76	.. {rbbc_store}
77	`«BUILD_ID»` from response `.metadata.build.id`
78	.. {rbbc_show}
79	Cloud Console URL for build monitoring
80	
81	//axhos_step
82	. *Cloud Build Step 1: Prepare Verification Keys*:
83	.. Image: alpine (from reliquary)
84	.. If conjure mode: write GCB attestor public key to `/workspace/keys/google-hosted-worker.pub`
85	.. If bind or graft mode: no-op (key not needed)
86	
87	//axhos_step
88	. *Cloud Build Step 2: Mode-Aware Verification* (Python 3):
89	.. Image: gcloud (from reliquary)
90	.. Fetch OAuth token from GCE metadata server
91	.. {rbbc_call}
92	Docker Registry API: GET manifests for `{gar_hallmarks_namespace}/«HALLMARK»/image:«HALLMARK»` to obtain OCI image index
93	.. Filter out attestation manifests before extracting platforms.
94	OCI image indexes may contain non-runnable attestation manifests (SLSA provenance, SBOMs) with platform `unknown/unknown`.
95	Exclude any manifest entry where _both_ `platform.os` and `platform.architecture` are `unknown`.
96	See: https://docs.docker.com/build/metadata/attestations/attestation-storage/[Docker Attestation Storage].
97	.. {rbbc_store}
98	`«PLATFORM_DIGESTS»` as list of `(platform, digest)` pairs from the remaining (non-attestation) manifest entries
99	.. {rbbc_store}
100	platform list to `/workspace/vouch_platforms.txt`
101	.. Branch on `_RBGV_VESSEL_MODE`:
102	+
103	**Conjure branch**:
104	.. For each `(platform, digest)` in `«PLATFORM_DIGESTS»`:
105	... {rbbc_call}
106	`gcloud artifacts docker images describe --show-provenance` to fetch DSSE envelope (provenance occurrences for the image digest)
107	... Extract payload (base64url), signature (base64url), and keyid from the DSSE envelope using Python `json` module
108	... Decode payload and signature via Python `base64.urlsafe_b64decode`
109	... Reconstruct Pre-Authentication Encoding (PAE): `DSSEv1 {payloadType_len} {payloadType} {payload_len} {payload}`
110	... {rbbc_call}
111	`openssl dgst -sha256 -verify «KEY_PEM» -signature «SIG_BIN» «PAE_BIN»` via subprocess, using attestor public key from step 1
112	... {rbbc_require}
113	signature verification succeeds -- proves GCB signed the provenance
114	... Read now-trusted provenance fields via Python `json`: `builder.id`, `buildType`, `invocationId`, subject digest
115	... {rbbc_store}
116	per-platform verification output to `/workspace/verify-«PLATFORM_SUFFIX».json`
117	.. {rbbc_require}
118	all platforms verify successfully
119	.. Compose `vouch_summary.json` via Python `json.dump`: `{hallmark, vessel, vessel_mode: "conjure", verify_method: "dsse-envelope", base_images: [...], platforms: [{verifier, builder_id, build_type, subject_digest, keyid, verdict: "pass", platform}, ...]}`
120	.. Record base image provenance: for each slot (1-3), if `_RBGV_IMAGE_«N»` is non-empty, append `{slot, ref, provenance}` to `base_images` array
121	+
122	**Bind branch**:
123	.. {rbbc_call}
124	Docker Registry API: HEAD manifests for `{gar_hallmarks_namespace}/«HALLMARK»/image:«HALLMARK»`
125	.. {rbbc_store}
126	`«ACTUAL_DIGEST»` from `Docker-Content-Digest` response header
127	.. Extract `«PINNED_DIGEST»` from the `@sha256:...` suffix of `_RBGV_BIND_SOURCE`
128	.. {rbbc_require}
129	`«ACTUAL_DIGEST»` equals `«PINNED_DIGEST»` -- pin integrity verified
130	.. Compose `vouch_summary.json`: `{hallmark, vessel, vessel_mode: "bind", verification: {method: "digest-pin", bind_source, actual_digest, pinned_digest, verdict: "DIGEST_PIN_VERIFIED"}}`
131	+
132	**Graft branch**:
133	.. Compose `vouch_summary.json`: `{hallmark, vessel, vessel_mode: "graft", verification: {method: "none", graft_source, verdict: "GRAFTED"}}`
134	.. No verification performed -- the GRAFTED verdict explicitly marks the absence of provenance chain
135	
136	//axhos_step
137	. *Cloud Build Step 3: Assemble and Push Vouch Artifact*:
138	.. Image: docker (from reliquary)
139	.. Create buildx builder instance (inspect-or-create pattern, same as conjure)
140	.. Build multi-platform `FROM scratch` container with summary and per-platform verification files as layers
141	.. Platforms: read from `/workspace/vouch_platforms.txt` (written by step 2 for all modes)
142	.. Push to registry at `{gar_hallmarks_namespace}/«HALLMARK»/vouch:«HALLMARK»` via `docker buildx build --push --tag ...`
143	.. Hallmark-only lookup by {rbtgo_ark_inspect} is served by subtree navigation — the vouch package lives directly under `{gar_hallmarks_namespace}/«HALLMARK»/`, so no cross-vessel aggregator tag is needed
144	.. {rbbc_require}
145	push succeeds
146	
147	//axhos_step
148	. *Wait for Build Completion* (local polling, not a GCB step):
149	.. The Director's workstation polls the builds.get API after submitting steps 1-3 as a single Cloud Build job
150	.. {rbbc_poll}
151	`GET «GCB_PROJECT_BUILDS_URL»/«BUILD_ID»` every 5 seconds
152	.. Continue while status in [`PENDING`, `QUEUED`, `WORKING`]
153	.. {rbbc_fatal}
154	if timeout or final status is not `SUCCESS` -- no partial vouch artifacts are pushed
155	
156	//axhoot_typed_output
157	{rbtga_ark_vouch}
158	. *Display Results*:
159	.. {rbbc_show}
160	vouch complete with hallmark and vouch artifact tag
161	.. {rbbc_show}
162	verdict type: `DSSE_VERIFIED` (conjure), `DIGEST_PIN_VERIFIED` (bind), or `GRAFTED` (graft)
163	
164	//axhoc_completion
165	Successful execution creates a
166	{rbtga_ark_vouch}
167	artifact in the registry at `{gar_hallmarks_namespace}/«HALLMARK»/vouch:«HALLMARK»`.
168	The vouch artifact is a multi-platform container (verification results are architecture-independent metadata; multi-platform is for pull ergonomics so arm64 hosts receive no platform mismatch warning).
169	+
170	*DSSE verification* (conjure only): Provenance is fetched via `gcloud artifacts docker images describe --show-provenance` as a DSSE envelope.
171	The verification script (Python 3) decodes base64url payload and signature, reconstructs PAE, and calls `openssl dgst -sha256 -verify` as a subprocess.
172	Attestor public keys are embedded in the step script (sourced from `projects/verified-builder/` KMS); no runtime KMS dependency.
173	The about/vouch steps run on the gcloud builder image and invoke `gcloud` directly (see {rbsk_no_gcloud} exception).
174	No jq, no slsa-verifier — Python 3 standard library + openssl only. Air-gap compatible (Private Google Access reaches Google APIs).
175	+
176	*Re-vouch idempotency*: Re-running vouch re-executes verification and pushes a new vouch artifact, overwriting the previous.
177	Content is presumed deterministic for conjure and bind; graft re-vouch always produces an identical GRAFTED stamp.
178	+
179	*Inspect integration*: Vouch results are displayed by {rbtgo_ark_inspect} in the Vouch Results section, showing per-platform DSSE verification verdicts (conjure), digest-pin confirmation (bind), or GRAFTED acknowledgment (graft).
180	
181	==== Vouch Substitution Variables
182	
183	All `_RBGV_*` substitution variables are passed in the `builds.create` request body.
184	
185	*Registry and identity*::
186	[cols="1,2"]
187	|===
188	| `_RBGV_GAR_HOST` | GAR Docker host (e.g., `us-central1-docker.pkg.dev`). Source: regime constants.
189	| `_RBGV_GAR_PATH` | GAR repository path (`«PROJECT»/«REPOSITORY»`). Source: regime constants.
190	| `_RBGV_VESSEL` | Vessel sigil. Source: loaded vessel configuration.
191	| `_RBGV_HALLMARK` | Full hallmark string. Source: input parameter.
192	| `_RBGV_VESSEL_MODE` | Vessel mode (`conjure`, `bind`, or `graft`). Source: loaded vessel configuration.
193	|===
194	
195	NOTE: Platform list is discovered by step 2 and written to `/workspace/vouch_platforms.txt`, not passed as a substitution variable.
196	Step 3 reads from workspace rather than re-discovering from the registry.
197	
198	*Mode-specific*::
199	[cols="1,2"]
200	|===
201	| `_RBGV_BIND_SOURCE` | Pinned upstream image reference (bind only). Source: {rbrv_bind_image}.
202	| `_RBGV_GRAFT_SOURCE` | Local image reference at push time (graft only). Source: recorded during {rbtgo_ark_graft}.
203	|===
204	
205	*Base image provenance* (conjure only, empty for other modes)::
206	[cols="1,2"]
207	|===
208	| `_RBGV_IMAGE_1` | Resolved base image reference for slot 1 (full GAR ref if anchored, upstream tag if pass-through). Empty if slot unused.
209	| `_RBGV_IMAGE_1_PROVENANCE` | Provenance type for slot 1: `anchored` (GAR-mirrored via enshrine) or `pass-through` (upstream direct). Empty if slot unused.
210	| `_RBGV_IMAGE_2` | Resolved base image reference for slot 2.
211	| `_RBGV_IMAGE_2_PROVENANCE` | Provenance type for slot 2.
212	| `_RBGV_IMAGE_3` | Resolved base image reference for slot 3.
213	| `_RBGV_IMAGE_3_PROVENANCE` | Provenance type for slot 3.
214	|===
215

file.content:
//axhob_operation
{rbtgo_ark_vouch}

NOTE: This operation produces the
{rbtga_ark_vouch}
artifact for an
{rbtga_ark}
in
{rbtgi_repository}
by running mode-aware verification in
{gcb_service}.
The
{rbtr_director}
submits a Cloud Build job via `builds.create` (direct submission), which runs as the
{rbtr_mason}
service account on the airgap private pool (`RBDC_POOL_AIRGAP`, derived from `RBDC_GCB_POOL_STEM` with `-airgap` suffix — no public egress).

NOTE: Vouch uses `builds.create` rather than trigger dispatch because the
{rbtga_hallmark}
is known only after the image arrives in GAR.
The private pool matches conjure's execution environment.

NOTE: The vouch verdict depends on vessel mode.
Conjure: DSSE envelope signature verification of GCB-signed provenance.
Bind: digest-pin comparison against the upstream reference.
Graft: GRAFTED stamp — explicit acknowledgment of no provenance chain.

NOTE: Vouch participates in the {rbsk_in_pool_preflight} only when embedded in graft's combined about+vouch Cloud Build job (`zrbfv_graft_metadata_submit`). The standalone vouch submission (`zrbfv_vouch_submit`, the post-image stage of conjure and bind ordain flows) is out of scope per the premise.

The
{rbtr_director}
vouches for an ark:

//axhopt_typed_parameter
{rbtga_ark}
. *Validate Input Parameters*:
.. {rbbc_require}
vessel provided as first argument
.. {rbbc_require}
hallmark provided as second argument

//axhos_step
. *Load Vessel Configuration*:
.. Source vessel configuration from `rbrv.env`
.. {rbbc_store}
`«VESSEL_MODE»` from {rbrv_vessel_mode}

//axhos_step
. *Authenticate as
{rbtr_director}*:
.. Execute
{rbtoe_director_authenticate}
.. {rbbc_store}
`«DIRECTOR_TOKEN»` for subsequent API calls

//axhos_step
. *Gate on About Existence*:
.. {rbbc_call}
Docker Registry API: HEAD manifests for `{gar_hallmarks_namespace}/«HALLMARK»/about:«HALLMARK»`
.. {rbbc_require}
HTTP 200 response -- {rbtgo_ark_about} must complete before vouch
.. If `{gar_hallmarks_namespace}/«HALLMARK»/vouch:«HALLMARK»` already exists:
{rbbc_warn}
re-vouch in progress (see idempotency below)

//axhos_step
. *Submit Vouch Cloud Build*:
.. Construct Build resource JSON with `serviceAccount` as
{rbtr_mason},
substitution namespace `_RBGV_*`
.. Pass registry coordinates, vessel mode, and verify method as substitution variables
.. {rbbc_submit}
`POST «GCB_PROJECT_BUILDS_URL»` with Build resource JSON
.. {rbbc_require}
HTTP 200 response
.. {rbbc_store}
`«BUILD_ID»` from response `.metadata.build.id`
.. {rbbc_show}
Cloud Console URL for build monitoring

//axhos_step
. *Cloud Build Step 1: Prepare Verification Keys*:
.. Image: alpine (from reliquary)
.. If conjure mode: write GCB attestor public key to `/workspace/keys/google-hosted-worker.pub`
.. If bind or graft mode: no-op (key not needed)

//axhos_step
. *Cloud Build Step 2: Mode-Aware Verification* (Python 3):
.. Image: gcloud (from reliquary)
.. Fetch OAuth token from GCE metadata server
.. {rbbc_call}
Docker Registry API: GET manifests for `{gar_hallmarks_namespace}/«HALLMARK»/image:«HALLMARK»` to obtain OCI image index
.. Filter out attestation manifests before extracting platforms.
OCI image indexes may contain non-runnable attestation manifests (SLSA provenance, SBOMs) with platform `unknown/unknown`.
Exclude any manifest entry where _both_ `platform.os` and `platform.architecture` are `unknown`.
See: https://docs.docker.com/build/metadata/attestations/attestation-storage/[Docker Attestation Storage].
.. {rbbc_store}
`«PLATFORM_DIGESTS»` as list of `(platform, digest)` pairs from the remaining (non-attestation) manifest entries
.. {rbbc_store}
platform list to `/workspace/vouch_platforms.txt`
.. Branch on `_RBGV_VESSEL_MODE`:
+
**Conjure branch**:
.. For each `(platform, digest)` in `«PLATFORM_DIGESTS»`:
... {rbbc_call}
`gcloud artifacts docker images describe --show-provenance` to fetch DSSE envelope (provenance occurrences for the image digest)
... Extract payload (base64url), signature (base64url), and keyid from the DSSE envelope using Python `json` module
... Decode payload and signature via Python `base64.urlsafe_b64decode`
... Reconstruct Pre-Authentication Encoding (PAE): `DSSEv1 {payloadType_len} {payloadType} {payload_len} {payload}`
... {rbbc_call}
`openssl dgst -sha256 -verify «KEY_PEM» -signature «SIG_BIN» «PAE_BIN»` via subprocess, using attestor public key from step 1
... {rbbc_require}
signature verification succeeds -- proves GCB signed the provenance
... Read now-trusted provenance fields via Python `json`: `builder.id`, `buildType`, `invocationId`, subject digest
... {rbbc_store}
per-platform verification output to `/workspace/verify-«PLATFORM_SUFFIX».json`
.. {rbbc_require}
all platforms verify successfully
.. Compose `vouch_summary.json` via Python `json.dump`: `{hallmark, vessel, vessel_mode: "conjure", verify_method: "dsse-envelope", base_images: [...], platforms: [{verifier, builder_id, build_type, subject_digest, keyid, verdict: "pass", platform}, ...]}`
.. Record base image provenance: for each slot (1-3), if `_RBGV_IMAGE_«N»` is non-empty, append `{slot, ref, provenance}` to `base_images` array
+
**Bind branch**:
.. {rbbc_call}
Docker Registry API: HEAD manifests for `{gar_hallmarks_namespace}/«HALLMARK»/image:«HALLMARK»`
.. {rbbc_store}
`«ACTUAL_DIGEST»` from `Docker-Content-Digest` response header
.. Extract `«PINNED_DIGEST»` from the `@sha256:...` suffix of `_RBGV_BIND_SOURCE`
.. {rbbc_require}
`«ACTUAL_DIGEST»` equals `«PINNED_DIGEST»` -- pin integrity verified
.. Compose `vouch_summary.json`: `{hallmark, vessel, vessel_mode: "bind", verification: {method: "digest-pin", bind_source, actual_digest, pinned_digest, verdict: "DIGEST_PIN_VERIFIED"}}`
+
**Graft branch**:
.. Compose `vouch_summary.json`: `{hallmark, vessel, vessel_mode: "graft", verification: {method: "none", graft_source, verdict: "GRAFTED"}}`
.. No verification performed -- the GRAFTED verdict explicitly marks the absence of provenance chain

//axhos_step
. *Cloud Build Step 3: Assemble and Push Vouch Artifact*:
.. Image: docker (from reliquary)
.. Create buildx builder instance (inspect-or-create pattern, same as conjure)
.. Build multi-platform `FROM scratch` container with summary and per-platform verification files as layers
.. Platforms: read from `/workspace/vouch_platforms.txt` (written by step 2 for all modes)
.. Push to registry at `{gar_hallmarks_namespace}/«HALLMARK»/vouch:«HALLMARK»` via `docker buildx build --push --tag ...`
.. Hallmark-only lookup by {rbtgo_ark_inspect} is served by subtree navigation — the vouch package lives directly under `{gar_hallmarks_namespace}/«HALLMARK»/`, so no cross-vessel aggregator tag is needed
.. {rbbc_require}
push succeeds

//axhos_step
. *Wait for Build Completion* (local polling, not a GCB step):
.. The Director's workstation polls the builds.get API after submitting steps 1-3 as a single Cloud Build job
.. {rbbc_poll}
`GET «GCB_PROJECT_BUILDS_URL»/«BUILD_ID»` every 5 seconds
.. Continue while status in [`PENDING`, `QUEUED`, `WORKING`]
.. {rbbc_fatal}
if timeout or final status is not `SUCCESS` -- no partial vouch artifacts are pushed

//axhoot_typed_output
{rbtga_ark_vouch}
. *Display Results*:
.. {rbbc_show}
vouch complete with hallmark and vouch artifact tag
.. {rbbc_show}
verdict type: `DSSE_VERIFIED` (conjure), `DIGEST_PIN_VERIFIED` (bind), or `GRAFTED` (graft)

//axhoc_completion
Successful execution creates a
{rbtga_ark_vouch}
artifact in the registry at `{gar_hallmarks_namespace}/«HALLMARK»/vouch:«HALLMARK»`.
The vouch artifact is a multi-platform container (verification results are architecture-independent metadata; multi-platform is for pull ergonomics so arm64 hosts receive no platform mismatch warning).
+
*DSSE verification* (conjure only): Provenance is fetched via `gcloud artifacts docker images describe --show-provenance` as a DSSE envelope.
The verification script (Python 3) decodes base64url payload and signature, reconstructs PAE, and calls `openssl dgst -sha256 -verify` as a subprocess.
Attestor public keys are embedded in the step script (sourced from `projects/verified-builder/` KMS); no runtime KMS dependency.
The about/vouch steps run on the gcloud builder image and invoke `gcloud` directly (see {rbsk_no_gcloud} exception).
No jq, no slsa-verifier — Python 3 standard library + openssl only. Air-gap compatible (Private Google Access reaches Google APIs).
+
*Re-vouch idempotency*: Re-running vouch re-executes verification and pushes a new vouch artifact, overwriting the previous.
Content is presumed deterministic for conjure and bind; graft re-vouch always produces an identical GRAFTED stamp.
+
*Inspect integration*: Vouch results are displayed by {rbtgo_ark_inspect} in the Vouch Results section, showing per-platform DSSE verification verdicts (conjure), digest-pin confirmation (bind), or GRAFTED acknowledgment (graft).

==== Vouch Substitution Variables

All `_RBGV_*` substitution variables are passed in the `builds.create` request body.

*Registry and identity*::
[cols="1,2"]
|===
| `_RBGV_GAR_HOST` | GAR Docker host (e.g., `us-central1-docker.pkg.dev`). Source: regime constants.
| `_RBGV_GAR_PATH` | GAR repository path (`«PROJECT»/«REPOSITORY»`). Source: regime constants.
| `_RBGV_VESSEL` | Vessel sigil. Source: loaded vessel configuration.
| `_RBGV_HALLMARK` | Full hallmark string. Source: input parameter.
| `_RBGV_VESSEL_MODE` | Vessel mode (`conjure`, `bind`, or `graft`). Source: loaded vessel configuration.
|===

NOTE: Platform list is discovered by step 2 and written to `/workspace/vouch_platforms.txt`, not passed as a substitution variable.
Step 3 reads from workspace rather than re-discovering from the registry.

*Mode-specific*::
[cols="1,2"]
|===
| `_RBGV_BIND_SOURCE` | Pinned upstream image reference (bind only). Source: {rbrv_bind_image}.
| `_RBGV_GRAFT_SOURCE` | Local image reference at push time (graft only). Source: recorded during {rbtgo_ark_graft}.
|===

*Base image provenance* (conjure only, empty for other modes)::
[cols="1,2"]
|===
| `_RBGV_IMAGE_1` | Resolved base image reference for slot 1 (full GAR ref if anchored, upstream tag if pass-through). Empty if slot unused.
| `_RBGV_IMAGE_1_PROVENANCE` | Provenance type for slot 1: `anchored` (GAR-mirrored via enshrine) or `pass-through` (upstream direct). Empty if slot unused.
| `_RBGV_IMAGE_2` | Resolved base image reference for slot 2.
| `_RBGV_IMAGE_2_PROVENANCE` | Provenance type for slot 2.
| `_RBGV_IMAGE_3` | Resolved base image reference for slot 3.
| `_RBGV_IMAGE_3_PROVENANCE` | Provenance type for slot 3.
|===

1	//axhob_operation
2	{bust_garrison_bash}
3	
4	NOTE: {busn_garrison} ceremony with native bash as the workload shell,
5	for Linux and macOS nodes.
6	Workload-only: garrison destroys any prior workload account named
7	{bujb_workload_user} via the letter-agnostic
8	`zbujb_obliterate_workload` helper in {bujb_jurisdiction} — the
9	single greppable destroy verb. On Linux the helper runs `userdel -r`
10	and purges `/home/<user>`; on macOS it runs `sysadminctl -deleteUser`
11	and purges `/Users/<user>`. Each step is idempotent on absent state.
12	Garrison never reads or writes any `sshd_config` and never modifies
13	admin authorized_keys.
14	
15	//axhopt_typed_parameter
16	{burp_investiture}
17	
18	//axhoq_precondition
19	A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
20	referenced {burn_regime} profile is reachable.
21	
22	//axhoq_precondition
23	sshd is reachable on the node and {burp_privileged_user} authenticates
24	by key alone using {burp_privileged_key_file}. On Linux/Mac admin
25	trust is established via {busn_caparison}, which wraps the
26	operator-managed `ssh-copy-id` flow rather than performing autonomous
27	SSH-trust hardening (only Windows OpenSSH has the autonomous
28	phase-1/phase-2 ceremony).
29	
30	//axhoq_precondition
31	{burp_workload_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
32	station.
33	
34	//axhos_step
35	. *Open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} using
36	{burp_privileged_key_file}; key-auth only. Failure here surfaces a
37	missing or stale admin trust.
38	
39	//axhos_step
40	. *Obliterate workload*: invoke `zbujb_obliterate_workload`. On Linux
41	the helper runs `sudo -n userdel -r` and `sudo -n rm -rf /home/<user>`;
42	on macOS it runs `sudo -n sysadminctl -deleteUser` and
43	`sudo -n rm -rf /Users/<user>`. Idempotent on absent state.
44	
45	//axhos_step
46	. *Create workload*: provision {bujb_workload_user} as an unprivileged,
47	ssh-only account.
48	
49	//axhos_step
50	. *Place workload trust*: write the workload pubkey
51	(derived via `ssh-keygen -y`) to the workload's authorized_keys with
52	the shell-letter `b` `command=` directive below — locked spec content,
53	mirrored at `BUJB_command_b` in {bujb_jurisdiction}:
54	+
55	[listing]
56	----
57	command="/bin/bash -lc \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\"",no-port-forwarding,no-X11-forwarding,no-agent-forwarding
58	----
59	
60	//axhos_step
61	. *Plant workload key*: copy {burp_workload_key_file} to the workload
62	account at the shell-letter `b` destination path hardcoded in
63	{bujb_jurisdiction}.
64	
65	//axhos_step
66	. *Validate round-trip*: SSH as workload, run a no-op command, verify
67	exit 0.
68	
69	//axhog_guarantee
70	On success the node has exactly one workload account named
71	{bujb_workload_user}, ssh-only, with both inbound (from operator) and
72	outbound (to GitHub or equivalent) key trust in place.
73	
74	//axhoc_completion
75	Exit 0 when the round-trip validation succeeds.
76	Non-zero on admin SSH failure, account-management failure, key-write
77	failure, or workload validation failure.
78

//axhob_operation
{bust_garrison_bash}

NOTE: {busn_garrison} ceremony with native bash as the workload shell,
for Linux and macOS nodes.
Workload-only: garrison destroys any prior workload account named
{bujb_workload_user} via the letter-agnostic
`zbujb_obliterate_workload` helper in {bujb_jurisdiction} — the
single greppable destroy verb. On Linux the helper runs `userdel -r`
and purges `/home/<user>`; on macOS it runs `sysadminctl -deleteUser`
and purges `/Users/<user>`. Each step is idempotent on absent state.
Garrison never reads or writes any `sshd_config` and never modifies
admin authorized_keys.

//axhopt_typed_parameter
{burp_investiture}

//axhoq_precondition
A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
referenced {burn_regime} profile is reachable.

//axhoq_precondition
sshd is reachable on the node and {burp_privileged_user} authenticates
by key alone using {burp_privileged_key_file}. On Linux/Mac admin
trust is established via {busn_caparison}, which wraps the
operator-managed `ssh-copy-id` flow rather than performing autonomous
SSH-trust hardening (only Windows OpenSSH has the autonomous
phase-1/phase-2 ceremony).

//axhoq_precondition
{burp_workload_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
station.

//axhos_step
. *Open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} using
{burp_privileged_key_file}; key-auth only. Failure here surfaces a
missing or stale admin trust.

//axhos_step
. *Obliterate workload*: invoke `zbujb_obliterate_workload`. On Linux
the helper runs `sudo -n userdel -r` and `sudo -n rm -rf /home/<user>`;
on macOS it runs `sudo -n sysadminctl -deleteUser` and
`sudo -n rm -rf /Users/<user>`. Idempotent on absent state.

//axhos_step
. *Create workload*: provision {bujb_workload_user} as an unprivileged,
ssh-only account.

//axhos_step
. *Place workload trust*: write the workload pubkey
(derived via `ssh-keygen -y`) to the workload's authorized_keys with
the shell-letter `b` `command=` directive below — locked spec content,
mirrored at `BUJB_command_b` in {bujb_jurisdiction}:
+
[listing]
----
command="/bin/bash -lc \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\"",no-port-forwarding,no-X11-forwarding,no-agent-forwarding
----

//axhos_step
. *Plant workload key*: copy {burp_workload_key_file} to the workload
account at the shell-letter `b` destination path hardcoded in
{bujb_jurisdiction}.

//axhos_step
. *Validate round-trip*: SSH as workload, run a no-op command, verify
exit 0.

//axhog_guarantee
On success the node has exactly one workload account named
{bujb_workload_user}, ssh-only, with both inbound (from operator) and
outbound (to GitHub or equivalent) key trust in place.

//axhoc_completion
Exit 0 when the round-trip validation succeeds.
Non-zero on admin SSH failure, account-management failure, key-write
failure, or workload validation failure.

content.content:
1	//axhob_operation
2	{bust_garrison_wsl}
3	
4	NOTE: {busn_garrison} ceremony with WSL bash as the workload shell,
5	for Windows nodes reached via the WSL distribution `rbtww-main`
6	(hardcoded in {bujb_jurisdiction}). On Windows the workload identity
7	inhabits *three namespaces*: a Windows local user (created via
8	`net.exe user /add` plus profile registration in
9	`HKLM\...\ProfileList`) provides the SSH authentication boundary that
10	Windows OpenSSH discovers; the workload user's *own* registered WSL
11	distribution (populated in their `HKCU\Lxss` via `wsl --import` from
12	a seed tarball) provides the distribution registration the workload's
13	session resolves; and a Linux user inside that workload-owned
14	distribution (created via `useradd`) provides the WSL execution
15	context entered by the locked-down `command=` directive. The third
16	namespace is forced by Microsoft's per-user WSL design: distributions
17	register only in the running user's `HKCU\Lxss`, and admin's
18	distributions are invisible to workload sessions.
19	
20	The mechanism is *SSH-as-workload conducted by the privileged
21	orchestrator*. The orchestrator never runs `wsl` as the workload
22	user directly. Instead it places a *bare* workload authorized_keys
23	entry (no `command=`), opens a workload SSH session through that
24	entry — which constitutes a real Windows logon for
25	{bujb_workload_user}, with HKCU mounted — and from inside that
26	session runs `wsl --import` (writing to the workload's HKCU), the
27	inner-WSL `useradd` + `passwd --lock`, and the workload privkey
28	placement. The orchestrator then replaces the bare entry with the
29	locked-down `command=` form and validates the round-trip through
30	that locked form.
31	
32	The Windows workload user is the same shape minted by the c branch
33	({bust_garrison_cygwin}); the workload-owned WSL distribution and
34	its inner Linux user are layered on top so the SSH session lands as
35	the named WSL user (via the locked-down `command=` directive's
36	`wsl.exe --user`) rather than the distribution's default user.
37	
38	Workload-only: garrison destroys any prior workload state across all
39	three namespaces via `zbujb_obliterate_workload` in
40	{bujb_jurisdiction} — the single greppable destroy verb. The
41	covenant scope spans the Windows SAM entry, the `C:\Users\<user>\`
42	Windows profile directory (by convention containing the workload's
43	WSL filesystem root, so purging the profile transitively destroys
44	the workload's WSL distribution registration's on-disk filesystem
45	and the HKCU hive that referenced it), the `C:\cygwin64\home\<user>\`
46	Cygwin home, and the WSL-side Linux user inside admin's `rbtww-main`
47	(vestige from prior garrison shapes; idempotent on absent state).
48	
49	Garrison never reads or writes any `sshd_config` and never modifies
50	admin authorized_keys; admin trust is established beforehand by
51	{busn_caparison} on the Windows OpenSSH server, while sshd hardening
52	inside the WSL distribution remains operator-manual.
53	
54	//axhopt_typed_parameter
55	{burp_investiture}
56	
57	//axhoq_precondition
58	A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
59	referenced {burn_regime} profile is reachable.
60	
61	//axhoq_precondition
62	{busn_caparison} has run for this {burp_investiture} so that
63	{burp_privileged_user} can authenticate by key alone using
64	{burp_privileged_key_file} into the Windows OpenSSH host (the admin
65	entry point); the workload SSH session is then opened from that
66	foothold using the workload pubkey just placed by garrison.
67	
68	//axhoq_precondition
69	{bust_invigilate_windows} passes for this {burp_investiture}: admin
70	SSH key trust is in place, the operator-managed registry posture is
71	correct, the Tailscale service is registered and set to Automatic,
72	powercfg sleep/hibernate are disabled, and the `rbtww-main` WSL
73	distribution (canonical name hardcoded as `BUJB_wsl_distribution` in
74	{bujb_jurisdiction}) is registered under the admin user. Garrison
75	runs invigilate as its first action so a missing or stale admin
76	posture surfaces with a fact-named diagnostic rather than as an
77	opaque downstream failure.
78	
79	//axhoq_precondition
80	{burp_workload_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
81	station.
82	
83	//axhos_step
84	. *Assert admin posture*: invoke `{bust_invigilate_windows}` for this
85	{burp_investiture}. Failure surfaces a fact-named diagnostic
86	({bust_handbook_windows} pointer for operator-managed facts;
87	{bust_caparison_windows} pointer for caparison-managed facts) and
88	aborts garrison before any destructive workload work.
89	
90	//axhos_step
91	. *Open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} using
92	{burp_privileged_key_file} into the Windows OpenSSH host; key-auth
93	only. Failure here surfaces a missing or stale admin trust.
94	
95	//axhos_step
96	. *Export seed*: export admin's `rbtww-main` distribution to a
97	workload-readable seed tarball under a path inside the workload's
98	profile (admin can write there before the workload first logs in,
99	and the workload's session can read it). Caparison has staged
100	`rbtww-main` and invigilate has verified its presence as a
101	precondition; this step takes that presence as given. The tarball
102	is the sole on-disk artifact carrying admin's `rbtww-main` contents
103	into the workload's identity scope; garrison removes it on success.
104	
105	//axhos_step
106	. *Obliterate workload*: invoke `zbujb_obliterate_workload`, the
107	letter-agnostic destroy helper. On Windows the helper executes a
108	single PowerShell session over admin SSH that purges the Windows
109	SAM entry (`net.exe user /delete`), the Windows profile directory
110	(`C:\Users\<user>\`, including any prior workload-owned WSL
111	filesystem under that path), the Cygwin home
112	(`C:\cygwin64\home\<user>\`), and the WSL-side Linux user inside
113	admin's `rbtww-main` (vestige from prior garrison shapes;
114	idempotent on absent state).
115	
116	//axhos_step
117	. *Create Windows workload account*: provision {bujb_workload_user}
118	as a Windows local user via
119	`net.exe user /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes` (matching the c
120	branch), then register the resulting SID under
121	`HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\ProfileList` so
122	Windows OpenSSH builds a complete logon session for the workload
123	on inbound SSH (Win32-OpenSSH issue #1383). No Linux user is
124	created inside admin's rbtww-main; the workload's Linux user is
125	minted inside the workload's *own* imported distribution in the
126	SSH-as-workload step below.
127	
128	//axhos_step
129	. *Place bare workload trust*: write the workload pubkey (derived
130	via `ssh-keygen -y` from {burp_workload_key_file}) to the
131	Windows-side authorized_keys at
132	`/mnt/c/Users/${BUJB_workload_user}/.ssh/authorized_keys` so Windows
133	OpenSSH discovers it natively. The directory is created with
134	`mkdir -p` and chmod 700; the file is written and chmod 600. No
135	POSIX `chown` is performed — `/mnt/c/...` ownership is meaningless
136	to Windows OpenSSH, which inspects NTFS ACLs; the inherited NTFS
137	ACL from the parent profile directory is admin-owned and read-only
138	for non-admins, satisfying StrictModes. The entry is *bare* — no
139	`command=` directive — because the locked-down `command=` would
140	route through a WSL distribution that has not yet been imported
141	under the workload's HKCU. The bare-then-lockdown pattern is
142	structural to this ceremony; the workload pubkey is derived from
143	{burp_workload_key_file} and owned by the operator, so the brief
144	window in which the bare entry is in place exposes only the
145	operator's own running garrison.
146	
147	//axhos_step
148	. *Initialize workload's WSL via SSH-as-workload*: open an SSH
149	session as {bujb_workload_user} using {burp_workload_key_file}
150	through the just-placed bare authorized_keys entry. This session
151	constitutes a real Windows logon for the workload, with HKCU
152	mounted. Inside that session, perform in order: `wsl.exe --import
153	rbtww-main <workload-wsl-root> <seed-tarball-path>` to register
154	the workload's own rbtww-main in `HKCU\Lxss`, where
155	`<workload-wsl-root>` is a workload-writable directory inside the
156	workload's Windows profile by convention;
157	`wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root useradd
158	--create-home --shell /bin/bash ${BUJB_workload_user}` to mint
159	the inner Linux workload user; `passwd --lock` (root inside the
160	distribution) on that Linux user to enforce SSH-key-only on the
161	inner account; and plant {burp_workload_key_file}'s contents at
162	`/home/${BUJB_workload_user}/.ssh/id_ed25519` (chmod 600) inside
163	the workload's distribution so the Linux session can use it for
164	outbound (e.g., GitHub). On exit, remove the seed tarball from
165	the admin side — the seed has served its purpose.
166	
167	//axhos_step
168	. *Lock down workload trust*: rewrite the workload's authorized_keys
169	file with the locked-down `command=` form, replacing the bare entry
170	placed earlier. The rewrite happens from a workload SSH session
171	because the file is workload-owned (per the icacls grants in step 5)
172	and the workload's session has direct write privilege without
173	ownership gymnastics. The directive content is locked spec text,
174	mirrored at `BUJB_command_w` in {bujb_jurisdiction};
175	`${BUJB_workload_user}` is substituted with the canonical workload
176	user at directive-emit time:
177	+
178	[listing]
179	----
180	command="C:/Windows/System32/wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user ${BUJB_workload_user} --exec /bin/bash -lc \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\"",no-port-forwarding,no-X11-forwarding,no-agent-forwarding
181	----
182	
183	//axhos_step
184	. *Validate round-trip*: SSH as workload, run a no-op command,
185	verify exit 0. Validates the *locked-down* form: the workload
186	session is routed through `command=`, which dispatches to the
187	workload's own imported `rbtww-main` distribution and runs the
188	inner Linux user's shell.
189	
190	//axhog_guarantee
191	On success the node has exactly one workload identity named
192	{bujb_workload_user} mirrored across all three namespaces: the
193	Windows local user (auth boundary, with profile registered in
194	`HKLM\...\ProfileList`), the workload-owned WSL distribution
195	`rbtww-main` (registered in workload's `HKCU\Lxss`), and the
196	WSL Linux user inside that distribution (execution context).
197	
198	//axhoc_completion
199	Exit 0 when the round-trip validation succeeds.
200	Non-zero on invigilate-windows assertion failure, admin SSH failure,
201	seed tarball export failure, account-management failure in any
202	namespace, `wsl --import` failure, key-write failure, or workload
203	validation failure.
204

file.content:
//axhob_operation
{bust_garrison_wsl}

NOTE: {busn_garrison} ceremony with WSL bash as the workload shell,
for Windows nodes reached via the WSL distribution `rbtww-main`
(hardcoded in {bujb_jurisdiction}). On Windows the workload identity
inhabits *three namespaces*: a Windows local user (created via
`net.exe user /add` plus profile registration in
`HKLM\...\ProfileList`) provides the SSH authentication boundary that
Windows OpenSSH discovers; the workload user's *own* registered WSL
distribution (populated in their `HKCU\Lxss` via `wsl --import` from
a seed tarball) provides the distribution registration the workload's
session resolves; and a Linux user inside that workload-owned
distribution (created via `useradd`) provides the WSL execution
context entered by the locked-down `command=` directive. The third
namespace is forced by Microsoft's per-user WSL design: distributions
register only in the running user's `HKCU\Lxss`, and admin's
distributions are invisible to workload sessions.

The mechanism is *SSH-as-workload conducted by the privileged
orchestrator*. The orchestrator never runs `wsl` as the workload
user directly. Instead it places a *bare* workload authorized_keys
entry (no `command=`), opens a workload SSH session through that
entry — which constitutes a real Windows logon for
{bujb_workload_user}, with HKCU mounted — and from inside that
session runs `wsl --import` (writing to the workload's HKCU), the
inner-WSL `useradd` + `passwd --lock`, and the workload privkey
placement. The orchestrator then replaces the bare entry with the
locked-down `command=` form and validates the round-trip through
that locked form.

The Windows workload user is the same shape minted by the c branch
({bust_garrison_cygwin}); the workload-owned WSL distribution and
its inner Linux user are layered on top so the SSH session lands as
the named WSL user (via the locked-down `command=` directive's
`wsl.exe --user`) rather than the distribution's default user.

Workload-only: garrison destroys any prior workload state across all
three namespaces via `zbujb_obliterate_workload` in
{bujb_jurisdiction} — the single greppable destroy verb. The
covenant scope spans the Windows SAM entry, the `C:\Users\<user>\`
Windows profile directory (by convention containing the workload's
WSL filesystem root, so purging the profile transitively destroys
the workload's WSL distribution registration's on-disk filesystem
and the HKCU hive that referenced it), the `C:\cygwin64\home\<user>\`
Cygwin home, and the WSL-side Linux user inside admin's `rbtww-main`
(vestige from prior garrison shapes; idempotent on absent state).

Garrison never reads or writes any `sshd_config` and never modifies
admin authorized_keys; admin trust is established beforehand by
{busn_caparison} on the Windows OpenSSH server, while sshd hardening
inside the WSL distribution remains operator-manual.

//axhopt_typed_parameter
{burp_investiture}

//axhoq_precondition
A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
referenced {burn_regime} profile is reachable.

//axhoq_precondition
{busn_caparison} has run for this {burp_investiture} so that
{burp_privileged_user} can authenticate by key alone using
{burp_privileged_key_file} into the Windows OpenSSH host (the admin
entry point); the workload SSH session is then opened from that
foothold using the workload pubkey just placed by garrison.

//axhoq_precondition
{bust_invigilate_windows} passes for this {burp_investiture}: admin
SSH key trust is in place, the operator-managed registry posture is
correct, the Tailscale service is registered and set to Automatic,
powercfg sleep/hibernate are disabled, and the `rbtww-main` WSL
distribution (canonical name hardcoded as `BUJB_wsl_distribution` in
{bujb_jurisdiction}) is registered under the admin user. Garrison
runs invigilate as its first action so a missing or stale admin
posture surfaces with a fact-named diagnostic rather than as an
opaque downstream failure.

//axhoq_precondition
{burp_workload_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
station.

//axhos_step
. *Assert admin posture*: invoke `{bust_invigilate_windows}` for this
{burp_investiture}. Failure surfaces a fact-named diagnostic
({bust_handbook_windows} pointer for operator-managed facts;
{bust_caparison_windows} pointer for caparison-managed facts) and
aborts garrison before any destructive workload work.

//axhos_step
. *Open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} using
{burp_privileged_key_file} into the Windows OpenSSH host; key-auth
only. Failure here surfaces a missing or stale admin trust.

//axhos_step
. *Export seed*: export admin's `rbtww-main` distribution to a
workload-readable seed tarball under a path inside the workload's
profile (admin can write there before the workload first logs in,
and the workload's session can read it). Caparison has staged
`rbtww-main` and invigilate has verified its presence as a
precondition; this step takes that presence as given. The tarball
is the sole on-disk artifact carrying admin's `rbtww-main` contents
into the workload's identity scope; garrison removes it on success.

//axhos_step
. *Obliterate workload*: invoke `zbujb_obliterate_workload`, the
letter-agnostic destroy helper. On Windows the helper executes a
single PowerShell session over admin SSH that purges the Windows
SAM entry (`net.exe user /delete`), the Windows profile directory
(`C:\Users\<user>\`, including any prior workload-owned WSL
filesystem under that path), the Cygwin home
(`C:\cygwin64\home\<user>\`), and the WSL-side Linux user inside
admin's `rbtww-main` (vestige from prior garrison shapes;
idempotent on absent state).

//axhos_step
. *Create Windows workload account*: provision {bujb_workload_user}
as a Windows local user via
`net.exe user /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes` (matching the c
branch), then register the resulting SID under
`HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\ProfileList` so
Windows OpenSSH builds a complete logon session for the workload
on inbound SSH (Win32-OpenSSH issue #1383). No Linux user is
created inside admin's rbtww-main; the workload's Linux user is
minted inside the workload's *own* imported distribution in the
SSH-as-workload step below.

//axhos_step
. *Place bare workload trust*: write the workload pubkey (derived
via `ssh-keygen -y` from {burp_workload_key_file}) to the
Windows-side authorized_keys at
`/mnt/c/Users/${BUJB_workload_user}/.ssh/authorized_keys` so Windows
OpenSSH discovers it natively. The directory is created with
`mkdir -p` and chmod 700; the file is written and chmod 600. No
POSIX `chown` is performed — `/mnt/c/...` ownership is meaningless
to Windows OpenSSH, which inspects NTFS ACLs; the inherited NTFS
ACL from the parent profile directory is admin-owned and read-only
for non-admins, satisfying StrictModes. The entry is *bare* — no
`command=` directive — because the locked-down `command=` would
route through a WSL distribution that has not yet been imported
under the workload's HKCU. The bare-then-lockdown pattern is
structural to this ceremony; the workload pubkey is derived from
{burp_workload_key_file} and owned by the operator, so the brief
window in which the bare entry is in place exposes only the
operator's own running garrison.

//axhos_step
. *Initialize workload's WSL via SSH-as-workload*: open an SSH
session as {bujb_workload_user} using {burp_workload_key_file}
through the just-placed bare authorized_keys entry. This session
constitutes a real Windows logon for the workload, with HKCU
mounted. Inside that session, perform in order: `wsl.exe --import
rbtww-main <workload-wsl-root> <seed-tarball-path>` to register
the workload's own rbtww-main in `HKCU\Lxss`, where
`<workload-wsl-root>` is a workload-writable directory inside the
workload's Windows profile by convention;
`wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root useradd
--create-home --shell /bin/bash ${BUJB_workload_user}` to mint
the inner Linux workload user; `passwd --lock` (root inside the
distribution) on that Linux user to enforce SSH-key-only on the
inner account; and plant {burp_workload_key_file}'s contents at
`/home/${BUJB_workload_user}/.ssh/id_ed25519` (chmod 600) inside
the workload's distribution so the Linux session can use it for
outbound (e.g., GitHub). On exit, remove the seed tarball from
the admin side — the seed has served its purpose.

//axhos_step
. *Lock down workload trust*: rewrite the workload's authorized_keys
file with the locked-down `command=` form, replacing the bare entry
placed earlier. The rewrite happens from a workload SSH session
because the file is workload-owned (per the icacls grants in step 5)
and the workload's session has direct write privilege without
ownership gymnastics. The directive content is locked spec text,
mirrored at `BUJB_command_w` in {bujb_jurisdiction};
`${BUJB_workload_user}` is substituted with the canonical workload
user at directive-emit time:
+
[listing]
----
command="C:/Windows/System32/wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user ${BUJB_workload_user} --exec /bin/bash -lc \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\"",no-port-forwarding,no-X11-forwarding,no-agent-forwarding
----

//axhos_step
. *Validate round-trip*: SSH as workload, run a no-op command,
verify exit 0. Validates the *locked-down* form: the workload
session is routed through `command=`, which dispatches to the
workload's own imported `rbtww-main` distribution and runs the
inner Linux user's shell.

//axhog_guarantee
On success the node has exactly one workload identity named
{bujb_workload_user} mirrored across all three namespaces: the
Windows local user (auth boundary, with profile registered in
`HKLM\...\ProfileList`), the workload-owned WSL distribution
`rbtww-main` (registered in workload's `HKCU\Lxss`), and the
WSL Linux user inside that distribution (execution context).

//axhoc_completion
Exit 0 when the round-trip validation succeeds.
Non-zero on invigilate-windows assertion failure, admin SSH failure,
seed tarball export failure, account-management failure in any
namespace, `wsl --import` failure, key-write failure, or workload
validation failure.

820	
821	[[busy_configurator]]
822	//axl_voices axo_entity
823	{busy_configurator}::
824	A `buyc_*` function called before kindle to set the formatting mode flag.
825	Three configurators exist: `buyc_dispatch` (probe TERM/NO_COLOR/BURD_*), `buyc_unconditional` (always color), `buyc_plain` (never color).
826	Kindle reads the mode flag and defines the readonly `BUYC_*` public color palette and hyperlink mode.
827	
828	== Control Voicings
829	
830	=== Bash Console Control Voicings
831	
832	Control terms for BUK operations executing in a bash shell with human operator present.
833	Each term voices the corresponding
834	{xref_AXLA}
835	control motif.
836	
837	[[busc_variable]]
838	//axl_voices axc_variable
839	{busc_variable}::
840	A shell variable denoted by guillemets `«NAME»` storing intermediate results.
841	Implemented as bash variables or associative array entries.
842	
843	[[busc_fatal]]
844	//axl_voices axc_fatal
845	{busc_fatal}::
846	Red console text followed by `exit 1`.
847	Script terminates immediately. Human operator must intervene.
848	
849	[[busc_warn]]
850	//axl_voices axc_warn
851	{busc_warn}::
852	Yellow console text indicating non-blocking concern.
853	Script continues execution. Human operator may note for later.
854	
855	[[busc_store]]
856	//axl_voices axc_store
857	{busc_store}::
858	Capture of a value from command output or API response into a
859	{busc_variable}.

A `buyc_*` function called before kindle to set the formatting mode flag.
Three configurators exist: `buyc_dispatch` (probe TERM/NO_COLOR/BURD_*), `buyc_unconditional` (always color), `buyc_plain` (never color).

content.text:
Here's the draft style brief. Pause point — let's discuss.

---

# Style Brief: BUSJ Mac/Linux Native Authoring (₢A-AA6)

## Heat context (for a cold-start subagent)
Recipe Bottle's `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUSJ*.adoc` family specifies jurisdiction operations (Caparison/Invigilate/Garrison/Handbook) per-platform. Four Windows files (BUSJIW/CW/HW/GC) just landed under a locked RBS prose discipline. Your task: author body content for the **mac/linux stubs** natively against that discipline. You are not translating existing prose; you are writing into stubs whose only content today is the NOTE preamble.

## Exemplar set (read these first to lock the style)

| Path | Role | What to mimic |
|------|------|----|
| `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUSJIW-InvigilateWindows.adoc` | Locked exemplar | Posture-fact-per-step, sparse voicings, single `«ADMIN_SESSION»`, em-dash fatals as cross-refs |
| `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUSJCW-CaparisonWindows.adoc` | Caparison sibling | Multi-step idempotent ceremony, mode-tagged «VAR»s, NOTEs decomposed |
| `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUSJHW-HandbookWindows.adoc` | Handbook sibling | `bush_*` voicings, literal commands in `[listing]` `----` blocks for copy-paste contract, UAC/dependency cautions via `bush_warn` |
| `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUSJGC-GarrisonCygwin.adoc` | Garrison sibling | Invigilate-first precondition pattern (not in scope here but useful for shape) |
| `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSAC-ark_conjure.adoc`, `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSAV-ark_vouch.adoc` | RBS reference | The original prose discipline — unvoiced `..` lines for plain descriptive actions, `{busc_use}` implicit |

## Prose discipline (locked across the family)

1. **One-line sub-bullets where the fact admits it.** A sub-bullet body is a single line. If you need two clauses, ride the second on an em-dash (`-- ...`), don't break to a second line.
2. **Voicings as sparse anchors, not decorative tags.** Pure descriptive actions (writes, lockdowns, idempotent ops without a captured value) ride unvoiced `..` lines. The RBSAC pattern — only call/store/require/fatal etc. when they carry weight.
3. **Trailing qualifiers ride `--`.** Em-dash clipped clauses, not subordinate "because/since/which" clauses. No why-clause on every action; sequencing rationale is implicit in step order.
4. **Fatal/warn bodies are short cross-refs, not paragraphs.** Canonical pattern: `-- {bust_handbook_macos} scope` or `-- aborting preserves the running sshd`. Two clauses maximum, em-dash separated.
5. **NOTE blocks: 1–3 sentence dense facts.** If you need to say four things, you need three NOTEs.
6. **Implicit `{busc_use}`.** The «VAR» reference inside a `{busc_require}` or following sub-step body IS the use. Don't pair `{busc_use}` next to `{busc_require}`.
7. **`axhog_guarantee` is a bullet list of asserted facts**, not a paragraph. Each bullet a single asserted post-condition.
8. **`axhoc_completion` is one or two sentences.** Exit 0 condition + non-zero conditions, terse.

**Self-test before submitting**: re-read each file with explanatory why-clauses removed. If the step still reads cleanly, the why-clause was non-load-bearing — drop it.

## Voicing distribution by file family

| Files | Voicings | Reference |
|-------|----------|-----------|
| BUSJCM, BUSJCL, BUSJIM, BUSJIL | `busc_*` (12 verbs: variable/fatal/warn/store/use/require/poll/call/submit/await/show/prompt) | Mirror BUSJIW + BUSJCW tag distribution |
| BUSJHM, BUSJHL | `bush_*` (5 verbs: fatal=STOP, warn=CAUTION, store=RECORD, require=VERIFY, show=NOTE) | Mirror BUSJHW + use `[listing]` / `----` blocks for literal commands |

## «VAR» repertoire

**Shared across family (use these names where the concept matches):**
- `«ADMIN_SESSION»` — admin SSH session handle (BUSJIW pattern)
- `«ADMIN_PUBKEY»` — `ssh-keygen -y` output on `{burp_privileged_key_file}` (BUSJCW pattern)
- `«INVIGILATE_RESULT»` — invigilate exit code (BUSJCW/BUSJGC pattern)

**Per-platform additions (mint as the posture-fact list demands; one «VAR» per fact, mirroring BUSJIW):**

macOS posture facts → suggested «VAR»s:
- `«REMOTE_LOGIN_STATE»` — `systemsetup -getremotelogin` output
- `«PMSET_REPORT»` — `pmset -g` output (sleep, displaysleep, hibernatemode)
- `«SVC_TAILSCALED_MAC»` — `launchctl list | grep tailscale` (or equivalent)

Linux posture facts → suggested «VAR»s:
- `«SSHD_SERVICE_STATE»` — `systemctl is-active sshd` / `is-enabled sshd`
- `«SLEEP_TARGETS_STATE»` — `systemctl status sleep.target suspend.target hibernate.target hybrid-sleep.target` (or equivalent)
- `«SVC_TAILSCALED_LINUX»` — `systemctl is-active tailscaled` / `is-enabled tailscaled`

Mint additional «VAR»s as the ceremony shape demands — one per captured fact. Don't introduce «VAR»s for transient values that aren't asserted against.

## Ceremony shapes (from paddock — the load-bearing decisions)

### Caparison-macOS (BUSJCM)
- Preconditions: BURP profile valid; BURN points at a macOS host; admin SSH key-auth works (operator handled ssh-copy-id per BUSJHM); privkey unencrypted.
- Steps (idempotent, nuclear posture; no skip-if-present):
  - Open admin SSH → store `«ADMIN_SESSION»`
  - Enable Remote Login: `systemsetup -setremotelogin on` over session
  - Apply `pmset`: disable sleep, displaysleep, hibernatemode (laptop and desktop)
  - Ensure Tailscale launchd is loaded + auto-start + running
  - Run invigilate-macos → store `«INVIGILATE_RESULT»` → require zero

### Caparison-Linux (BUSJCL)
- Preconditions: parallel to macOS; BURN points at a Linux host; admin SSH key-auth works (operator handled ssh-copy-id per BUSJHL).
- Steps:
  - Open admin SSH → `«ADMIN_SESSION»`
  - Ensure sshd enabled+running via systemctl (idempotent)
  - Mask sleep/suspend/hibernate/hybrid-sleep targets via systemctl
  - Enable+start tailscaled via systemctl
  - Run invigilate-linux → `«INVIGILATE_RESULT»` → require zero

### Invigilate-macOS (BUSJIM)
- Preconditions: BURP profile valid; BURN points at macOS; admin SSH reachable as key-only.
- Steps: one posture-fact assertion per step. First step opens admin SSH (matches BUSJIW first step). Subsequent steps: call read → store «VAR» → require expected → fatal cross-ref pointer to `{bust_handbook_macos}` (operator-managed) or `{bust_caparison_macos}` (caparison-managed).
- Posture facts to cover: admin SSH (call+store fatal-on-failure pointer to caparison), Remote Login on, pmset values, Tailscale launchd loaded+running. (Add facts only if they're testable read-only and load-bearing.)

### Invigilate-Linux (BUSJIL)
- Parallel to Invigilate-macOS.
- Posture facts: admin SSH, sshd active+enabled, sleep-targets masked, tailscaled active+enabled.

### Handbook-macOS (BUSJHM)
- Operator-manual scope; no remote contact, no profile mutation.
- `bush_*` voicings throughout. Literal commands in `[listing]` `----` blocks.
- Steps to cover (each step its own `//axhos_step`):
  - Install Tailscale (App Store or pkg from tailscale.com)
  - First-run Tailscale auth (sign in via menu-bar app)
  - Ensure the admin user has a known password (for first-run ssh-copy-id)
  - First-run `ssh-copy-id` to place admin pubkey
  - Configure sudoers NOPASSWD entry for `{burp_privileged_user}` if the privileged ceremony requires sudo
  - Enable Remote Login (System Settings → General → Sharing → Remote Login) — _operator alternative to caparison's `systemsetup -setremotelogin on` for the first run_
- Cautions via `bush_warn` for irreversible-feeling actions (sudoers edits, Privacy & Security prompts).

### Handbook-Linux (BUSJHL)
- Operator-manual scope; no remote contact.
- `bush_*` voicings + `[listing]` blocks.
- Steps:
  - Install openssh-server on minimal distros (`apt install openssh-server` / `dnf install openssh-server`)
  - Install Tailscale per-distro (`curl -fsSL https://tailscale.com/install.sh | sh` or distro pkg)
  - First-run `tailscale up`
  - Ensure admin user has known password (for first-run ssh-copy-id)
  - First-run `ssh-copy-id`
  - Configure sudoers NOPASSWD entry for `{burp_privileged_user}`
- Cautions via `bush_warn` for sudoers edits (visudo, syntax mistakes lock out admin).

## Linked-term inventory (all minted in BUS0 — reference with `{name}`)

Operations: `{bust_caparison_macos}` `{bust_caparison_linux}` `{bust_invigilate_macos}` `{bust_invigilate_linux}` `{bust_handbook_macos}` `{bust_handbook_linux}` `{bust_garrison_bash}`
Notions: `{busn_caparison}` `{busn_invigilate}` `{busn_garrison}`
BURP/BURN: `{burp_investiture}` `{burp_regime}` `{burp_privileged_user}` `{burp_privileged_key_file}` `{burp_workload_key_file}` `{burn_regime}`
BUJB: `{bujb_jurisdiction}` `{bujb_workload_user}`
Voicings: `{busc_*}` (12), `{bush_*}` (5) — see BUS0 §Control Voicings lines 830–953

## Out of scope (do not edit)
- BUS0 vocabulary
- Bash code (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh`, etc.)
- Any BUSJ file not in your pair
- BUSJGB (main thread handles this)

## Done-when (per pair)
- Both files in pair land as siblings of the listed exemplars (sparse voicings, em-dash qualifiers, bounded NOTEs, bullet-list guarantees).
- Every `{name}` reference resolves to a BUS0 mapping entry.
- Structure: `//axhob_operation` opener, optional NOTEs, `//axhopt_typed_parameter`, one or more `//axhoq_precondition`, one or more `//axhos_step`, `//axhog_guarantee`, `//axhoc_completion`.
- No bash code edits, no BUS0 edits.

---

content.text:
**Pausing for discussion.** A few things I'm flagging that you might want to push back on:

1. **«VAR» repertoire suggestions are speculative** — I named candidates from the paddock's ceremony descriptions, but the subagents will mint their own as the fact list demands. I could either (a) leave them as suggestions like above, (b) lock specific «VAR» names here as the cross-pair coordination contract, or (c) drop the suggestions entirely and let each pair mint.

2. **Invigilate fact lists are open** — I gave indicative posture facts but didn't lock the count. BUSJIW has 7 facts; if mac/linux land with 3 facts they'll read leaner than the Windows sibling — is that drift, or is it correct (Windows genuinely has more posture to verify)? I lean toward "correct, don't pad."

3. **Caparison-{mac,linux} have no phase structure** — unlike Windows (3 phases). The paddock describes them as "compact" single-phase ceremonies. The exemplar shape is therefore BUSJCW *without* the phase numbering — closer to BUSJGC's flat step list. I implied this but didn't make it loud.

4. **Handbook content is the softest area** — operator-manual steps depend on what the operator actually has to do, which depends on distro/OS-version variability. I could have the subagents err on the side of canonical commands (Ubuntu/Debian for Linux; current macOS for mac) and accept that distros with `dnf`/`zypper` get a `bush_show` aside.

5. **Single brief vs. three tailored briefs** — currently this is one brief that all three subagents read, with their pair scope as the only differentiation. Alternative: one shared discipline doc + three short per-pair prompts. Lower duplication, but each subagent has to read two artifacts instead of one. I think the single brief is fine; subagents are not bandwidth-constrained.

What would you like to redirect on?

content.text:
You're right — most of that brief restates things a subagent would pick up by reading the exemplars and the commit messages on the recent ABA work. Let me strip it.

**Remaining work, simply:**

1. **Three parallel subagents write six files.** Each subagent reads BUSJIW/CW/HW/GC (the four ABA-landed exemplars) and writes one pair of stubs to look like the matching exemplar. Pair 1: BUSJCM + BUSJCL (match BUSJCW shape). Pair 2: BUSJIM + BUSJIL (match BUSJIW shape). Pair 3: BUSJHM + BUSJHL (match BUSJHW shape). The paddock has the per-platform ceremony content; the exemplars have the form.

2. **I align BUSJGB.** Add an Assert-admin-posture first step + reference `{bust_invigilate_macos}` / `{bust_invigilate_linux}` in the precondition list, mirroring BUSJGW.

3. **Run two gates.** `tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh` and `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh`.

The load-bearing parts of the brief are: which exemplar pairs with which stub, which voicing family (busc_/bush_), the out-of-scope list, and the per-platform ceremony content from the paddock. The rest — the discipline rules, the «VAR» suggestions, the linked-term inventory — is either already in the exemplars or self-evident from a grep. I'll cut those when I write the actual subagent prompts.

Does that match what you had in mind, or were you flagging that the work itself has grown beyond what the pace should be?

The handbook is what the operator does **before** caparison can run — the security-sensitive, interactive, or operator-by-policy steps that caparison cannot or should not automate. No remote contact. No profile mutation. Renders to operator's terminal as procedural prose with copy-pasteable commands.

**BUSJHM (Handbook macOS):** Operator-manual posture for a macOS host. Steps to cover (each `//axhos_step`):
1. Install Tailscale — App Store version or pkg from https://tailscale.com/download . Verify Tailscale.app present in /Applications.
2. First-run Tailscale auth — open Tailscale menu-bar icon, click Sign In, complete browser-based auth flow. Verify host appears in operator's tailnet admin console.
3. Ensure `{burp_privileged_user}` has a known password — needed for first-run `ssh-copy-id`. Set via System Settings → Users & Groups or `dscl . -passwd /Users/<user> <newpassword>` if necessary.
4. First-run `ssh-copy-id` — from operator's station, run `ssh-copy-id -i <pubkey-path> {burp_privileged_user}@<host>`. Operator types the privileged-user password once. Verify subsequent key-auth ssh works.
5. Configure sudoers NOPASSWD entry — if `{burp_privileged_user}` is not already a passwordless-sudo user, add `<user> ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: ALL` via `sudo visudo`. `bush_warn` for syntax mistakes locking out admin.
6. Verify Remote Login is enableable — System Settings → General → Sharing → Remote Login (operator can either enable here as a first-run alternative or let caparison's `systemsetup -setremotelogin on` handle it). `bush_show` noting caparison will (re)assert this.

**BUSJHL (Handbook Linux):** Operator-manual posture for a Linux host. Steps:
1. Install openssh-server — `sudo apt install openssh-server` (Debian/Ubuntu) or `sudo dnf install openssh-server` (Fedora/RHEL) or distro equivalent. `bush_show` aside listing common variants. Verify `which sshd` returns a path.
2. Install Tailscale — `curl -fsSL https://tailscale.com/install.sh | sh` (cross-distro one-liner) or per-distro package. Verify `tailscale version` reports.
3. First-run `tailscale up` — `sudo tailscale up` opens an auth URL; operator authenticates in browser. Verify host appears in operator's tailnet admin console.
4. Ensure `{burp_privileged_user}` has a known password — needed for first-run `ssh-copy-id`. Set via `sudo passwd <user>` if necessary.
5. First-run `ssh-copy-id` — from operator's station, run `ssh-copy-id -i <pubkey-path> {burp_privileged_user}@<host>`. Operator types the privileged-user password once. Verify subsequent key-auth ssh works.
6. Configure sudoers NOPASSWD entry — `sudo visudo` and add `<user> ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: ALL`. `bush_warn` for syntax mistakes locking out admin (`visudo` validates before saving, but warn explicitly).
